title: Understanding Forces
description: A force is a push or pull that can change the motion of an object. It has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity.  Explore Newton’s laws, inertia, friction, gravity, and dark energy to understand motion from everyday objects to galaxies and the expanding universe.
learning_objectives: Explain Newton’s laws of motion and apply them to describe how forces affect the motion of objects on Earth and in space.
Investigate inertia and mass to understand resistance to changes in motion, including real-life examples such as vehicles, friction, and safety devices.
Analyze the role of friction as a force opposing motion, and compare how different surfaces (e.g., ice, concrete) affect the speed and stopping of moving objects.
Construct and interpret free-body diagrams to represent balanced and unbalanced forces, net force, and predict resulting motion.
Apply Newton’s third law of motion to everyday and scientific contexts, such as swimming, walking, rockets, and planetary interactions.
Explore gravitational force as described by Newton’s Law of Universal Gravitation, and use it to compare motions of celestial bodies and planetary orbits.
Discuss current scientific ideas about galaxies, dark matter, and dark energy, connecting them to Newtonian mechanics and the expansion of the universe.

title: Work, Energy, and Energy Resources
description: In this pathway, energy is loosely defined as the ability to do work, admitting that in some circumstances not all energy is available to do work. Because of the association of energy with work, we begin the pathway with a discussion of work. Work is intimately related to energy and how energy moves from one system to another or changes form. The work-energy theorem, introduced in this pathway, establishes the relationship between work done on an object by an external force and changes in the object’s kinetic energy. Similarly, systems can do work on each other. This pathway also discusses how to calculate kinetic, gravitational, and elastic potential energy in order to determine the total mechanical energy of a system; and the conservation of energy.
learning_objectives: Explain how an object must be displaced for a force on it to do work.
Explain how relative directions of force and displacement of an object determine whether the work done on the object is positive, negative, or zero.
Explain work as a transfer of energy and net work as the work done by the net force.
Explain and apply the work-energy theorem.
Explain gravitational potential energy in terms of work done against gravity.
Describe that the gravitational potential energy of an object of mass m at height h on earth is given by peg = mgh.
Describe how knowledge of potential energy as a function of position can be used to simplify calculations and explain physical phenomena.
Define conservative force, potential energy, and mechanical energy.
Explain the potential energy of a spring in terms of its compression when hooke’s law applies.
Use the work-energy theorem to show how having only conservative forces leads to conservation of mechanical energy.
Define nonconservative forces and explain how they affect mechanical energy.

Describe how the principle of conservation of energy can be applied by treating the conservative forces in terms of their potential energies and any nonconservative forces in terms of the work they do.
Explain the law of the conservation of energy.
Describe some of the many forms of energy.
Define efficiency of an energy conversion process as the fraction left as useful energy or work, rather than being transformed, for example, into thermal energy.
Calculate power by calculating changes in energy over time.
Examine power consumption and calculations of the cost of energy consumed.
Explain the human body’s consumption of energy when at rest versus when engaged in activities that do useful work.
Calculate the conversion of chemical energy in food into useful work.
Describe the distinction between renewable and nonrenewable energy sources.
Explain why the inevitable conversion of energy to less useful forms makes it necessary to conserve energy resources.

title: Living in a Material World: The Importance of Materials Science and Engineering
description: Interested in the science of making, characterizing, and testing materials? Materials Science and Engineering (MSE) explores the relationship between a material structure and properties on all levels and seeks to develop new materials and applications through innovative and creative thinking.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of testing materials and how this can inform new materials' development.
You will learn the significance of a material's mechanical and chemical properties.
You will understand the importance of microstructure in determining a material's properties.
You will have some insight into the career opportunities associated with Materials Science.
